name: scenario_109f5d_criminal_procedure_communications_outage_cedar_lib_eq
premise: the incident command structure is under pressure in Q4 2026: 5 critical issues have surfaced over 6 hours, and the response will set a policy precedent for Criminal Procedure. The immediate incident is a communications outage. The trigger is that a communications outage is forcing fallback workflows, and the fallback is less safe but currently the only way to operate. The board has pre-approved up to $3.0M in emergency spend if controls are documented, reversible, and auditable. Stakeholders frame this as liberty vs equality: the choice will set a precedent under pressure and incomplete evidence. Dashboards conflict with frontline notes, and vendor telemetry is delayed enough that decisions must be made on partial evidence. A decision is required before a 24-hour disclosure deadline. Boundaries: The response must include an appeals path usable without legal counsel. No one is denied service solely due to an automated flag; a human fallback must exist. If the vendor cannot provide logs within 2 hours, the plan must assume worst-case exposure. Whatever choice is made will be cited in later incidents as the default playbook for this domain.

agents.name: Program Chair Elliot Bennett
agents.identity: I am Program Chair Elliot Bennett, coordinating the Criminal Procedure response at Cedar Criminal Procedure Coordination Cell. My mandate is to act fast without blurring accountability, to keep an auditable trail, and to make sure the costs of errors don’t land on people least able to contest them.
agents.goal: I want a decision that is bounded, reversible, and measurable—so we can move quickly without losing the ability to stop.

agents.name: Patient Safety Liaison Marco Kaur
agents.identity: I am Patient Safety Liaison Marco Kaur, coordinating the Criminal Procedure response at Cedar Criminal Procedure Coordination Cell. My mandate is to act fast without blurring accountability, to keep an auditable trail, and to make sure the costs of errors don’t land on people least able to contest them.
agents.goal: I want transparency that separates verified facts from guesses and forces equitable review of who is harmed by defaults.

choices: I will authorize a narrow, time-boxed pilot first, with a written exception policy, weekly subgroup error-rate disclosure, and two-person sign-off for overrides; I will not scale until predefined safety and equity checks pass.
I will authorize a broad rollout immediately to reduce near-term harm, with transparent metrics and a rapid correction loop, accepting that some harms may surface only after precedent is set.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The window for a controlled decision is closing. Program Chair Elliot Bennett must choose an authorization approach for this Criminal Procedure decision. What does Program Chair Elliot Bennett do?
